data.title: Aflac: Steady Compounding At Fair Value
data.description:
Aflac (NYSE:AFL) is one of the most well-known insurance providers, and over time, it's proved to be one of the best investments.

Over the past decade, AFL has massively outperformed the S&P 500. While I think the underlying business is solid, and able to maintain dividends and buybacks, Aflac has no easy path to growth and trades at a historically high P/E ratio. For that reason, I rate the stock a Hold.

Financial History

What's interesting about the total returns Aflac has produced is that it's happened in the absence of growth in the purest sense.

Looking at historical revenue, we can see this. In fact, 2023 and 2024 were the two lowest years of the past decade as far as revenue is concerned. As the latter half of the decade is really the period in which AFL outpaced the market, this is additionally surprising.

Much of this is better explained by how much Aflac has improved its earnings over time as well. Despite flat-to-shrinking revenue, earnings have more than doubled in this period. Premiums from their insurance products consistently exceed benefits paid, underwriting costs, and other operating expenses.

Never having to take a loss, this also means the investment income from their portfolio also continues to grow, unimpeded.

The last key trend to observe is how cash is returned to shareholders. While dividends have steadily increased as earnings grow, buybacks have been the main way this company enhances the intrinsic value of each share. Total shares outstanding shrank from 848M in 2015 to 550M at the end of 2024.

It therefore isn't too surprising that AFL has been such a solid investment for total returns, and it gives an idea of the right questions to ask about the future. Before we do that, let's look at the operations that support these results.

Business Model

Aflac's operations are broken into two segments:

  • Aflac Japan
  • Aflac U.S.

For several years, Aflac Japan has been the largest contributor to revenue, but its role has been steadily declining over the years, only contributing 55% of revenue in 2024 (69% in 2020). While they offer similar types of insurance, they are not identical businesses, reflecting subtleties between the Japanese and American markets. Aflac Japan's products include:

  • Cancer (about 14M of the segment's 22M policies in force)
  • Medical/Health
  • Nursing
  • Various life products

As Japan is a wealthy nation known for its aging population, these are products that generally support customers into old age. Aflac U.S. underwrites the following:

  • Accident
  • Disability
  • Critical Care (includes cancer)
  • Hospital Indemnity
  • Dental & Vision
  • Life

As their core markets evolve, it's largely a matter of adjusting their product mix accordingly and maintaining a strong marketing and distribution process. The duck does the marketing, while distribution is handled by third parties. Often this is other insurance companies, but in Japan, banks also play a role in insurance sales.

Lastly, the table above shows how Aflac's assets are invested. The vast majority of these are in investment-grade, fixed-income assets, and Aflac's total revenues are supported by the income from this portfolio.

Future Outlook

Whether or not AFL makes for a good investment going forward depends on our feelings about the future of their business, their capital allocation, and the value we get for the shares. As life and related insurance products go, the formula is easy. They simply collect premiums over a long enough period of time that, when invested, provide more than enough to cover the benefits and claims.

I'm not worried about Aflac's ability to do that. Even though everyone gets sick and dies at some point, it's proved to be a stable, dependable business. Life insurance in general is known for this.

CEO Daniel Amos also spoke optimistically about growth in both segments during the Q1 earnings call:

We believe in the underlying strength of our business and our potential for continued growth in Japan and the United States, two of the largest life insurance markets in the world. On an ongoing basis, we are taking action to reinforce our leading position and build on our momentum.

My main concern is more so in the lack of growth. Japan's aging population is one indication of this, and the national total has been in decline since 2010. Despite management's ambitions, I fear that Aflac Japan will be on a long-term trend of run-offs and working to maintain market share unless something changes in this country. The historical results, with Aflac Japan's contribution to revenue declining, seem to bear this out already.

Their latest Form 10Q indicates that they have about 22 million policies in force currently. Japan is definitely large enough technically they could have eight times as many policies, but they'd have to overcome competition. Major insurers, such as Dai-ichi and Japan Post, provide key distribution services as partners with Aflac, but there's only so much of their lunch that Aflac could eat before they would have to lose this advantage. There's just no clear path to growth, even if management has that never-say-never attitude.

Aflac U.S. is different, as the American population isn't declining. If we look at their Q1 market share in the U.S., it's still competitive but also fragmented. Aflac ranks 7th in accident and health, with a 2.15% market share. Growth seems more possible here than in Japan, but how likely is it?

While property-casualty insurers can grow over time with superior underwriting (punishing competitors who underwrite losses and lose initiative), life and similar products are easier to do profitably, as I explained before. Consequently, I think it's harder to gain a long-term advantage. A superior sales strategy is really the only way to grow and preserve margin. The Aflac duck did this over 20 years ago for both Japan and the U.S. The company itself claims that brand recognition grew from 11% to 94%. That was a game-changer then, but what could possibly replicate that now?

Therein lies my concern that Aflac is only a slow grower in a best-case scenario. The capital allocation, with nearly all earnings spent on share repurchases and dividends, indicates this. The buybacks are a safe way to "invest in the business" without actually having to put it to work.

Yes, earnings have grown over time with expense improvements and rising portfolio income. The buybacks also help. Diluted EPS grew from $2.79 in 2015 to $7.10 in 2024. These are very limited in how much they can grow EPS, though.

With revenues shrinking as Japan ages and the buybacks occurring at historically high multiples, AFL simply isn't compounding at the same rate of return investors got a decade ago.

If we look at AFL's valuation, we see that its returns are a product of multiple expansions too. At a P/E of 15, this is some of the richest AFL has ever been valued. I think this largely occurred as investors moved to "defensive" stocks like insurance in 2022, responding to Russia's invasion of Ukraine and the Fed's interest rate hikes. That is to say, nothing about Aflac specifically requires it to be valued like this.

AFL has the signs of being a slow-and-steady kind of stock. This is especially true if we get it at a PEG ratio that favors such an outcome. Since it isn't there anymore, I think it's more realistic to consider AFL fairly valued. It isn't terribly risky, and its dividend, usually in a payout ratio of 20% to 30%, is well-covered. I just think the current numbers support much flatter returns over time than the previous decade offered, and I'd prefer a P/E under 10 before buying.

Conclusion

Aflac isn't doomed, but I think we should be realistic about what it can deliver. It's normal for management to beat the drums and talk about plans for growth. They talked about this in 2020 earnings calls. It's their job to look for opportunities and not give up. We have to look at what they actually do with most of their earnings. They give it to shareholders.

Why? Because talking about growth is easier said than done. Life and related products aren't hard to underwrite profitably, but most of the earnings aren't going back into this. That's why I raise my eyebrows at a P/E of 15 for such a company. It's not a bubble valuation, but it's not a bargain either, and that's why I give AFL a Hold rating today.

data.title: Generative AI Experiences Rapid Adoption, But With Mixed Outcomes - Highlights From VotE: AI And Machine Learning
data.description:
Introduction

Adoption of generative AI has exceeded even the ambitious strategies set by organizations in 2024. The technology has been shifting from experimentation to operational implementation, although many organizations are still trying to fully scale up deployments. However, the organizational impact of these projects has not progressed as quickly as their increased implementation. Voice of the Enterprise: AI & Machine Learning, Use Cases 2025 — a survey of 1,006 midlevel and senior IT and line-of-business professionals across North America and Europe — reveals that despite high enthusiasm and investment, many organizations are not seeing much more benefit than when they were just experimenting with the technology.

The Take

Generative AI is delivering impact to organizations. The growing question is whether this impact measures up against organizational investments, as well as the opportunity cost. The commonly held view has been that this cost/benefit balance would shift in favor of generative AI as model performance improved and organizations began integrating generative AI into practices. Instead, this year's data indicates that fewer organizations are seeing positive impacts from generative AI, and in many cases, AI projects' performance against KPIs is stagnating. Higher reported project failure rates, likely driven by the accelerated approach to generative AI adoption, is another worrying indicator as to how these investments are performing. However, a cohort of high-performance organizations are defying this broader pattern with a more mature monitoring and tailoring strategy, a more holistic approach to project prioritization, and a better handle on security, bias and data privacy challenges.

Summary of findings

The rate of generative AI adoption outstrips longer-standing forms of AI, as well as enterprise forecasts. Most organizations that are actively investing in AI have generative AI in production. Of these, 27% say they have organization-wide adoption, while 33% say it is limited to specific departments or projects. This marks an increase from the year-ago survey figures of 13% and 28%, respectively. The adoption rate surpasses that of pattern-based and rules-based AI initiatives and exceeds the forecasts organizations made in 2024. By the end of 2025, 40% expect organization-wide rollout of generative AI tools.

Project failure rates appear to be elevated, as organizations attempt to deliver generative AI projects at pace. The percentage of companies abandoning the majority of their AI initiatives before they reach production has surged from 17% to 42% year-over-year, with organizations on average reporting that 46% of projects are scrapped between proof of concept and broad adoption. This marks a significant departure from the previous downward trend, highlighting the difficulties many organizations face in rapidly deploying AI projects. Companies with higher project failure rates are notably more prone to encountering resistance from customers and employees, and have a greater level of concern surrounding reputational damage. Organizations with a lower project failure rate have a more holistic approach to project prioritization and are comparatively more likely to consider compliance, risk and data availability criteria when selecting projects.

Fewer organizations report positive outcomes from generative AI, but some are finding value in unexpected areas. The proportion of organizations citing a positive impact from generative AI investments has fallen across every enterprise objective assessed. While the majority of organizations still perceive a positive impact, the latest survey reflects a notable year-over-year drop in areas such as revenue growth objectives (76%, down from 81%), cost management (74%, down from 79%) and risk management (70%, down from 74%). These declines stand out because many expected the performance of generative AI initiatives to increase as the technology matured, and as organizations moved from experimenting with tools to embedding them into processes. Concerningly, among respondents whose organizations had invested in generative AI, 46% reported that no single enterprise objective had seen a "strong positive impact" from that investment. Considering the investments that organizations have made into generative AI, and the clear opportunity costs, the performance of these applications is concerning.

A more positive story is emerging around some generative AI application areas that fewer organizations were targeting or deriving value from a year ago. Notably, internal system interaction, complex multistep processes such as design and prototyping, and sales outreach appear to be associated with higher levels of enterprise impact than popular areas such as content creation or information summarization. Because these more complex, task-based challenges are closely associated with "agentic" frameworks, these application areas may see a further shot in the arm in the coming year.

AI agents are perceived as a key to unlocking value with generative AI. In the face of these lower-than-expected benefits, the emerging paradigm of agentic AI is prompting renewed enthusiasm in its potential for value creation. Interest is nearly universal: 58% of respondents say they are seeking opportunities to implement agents and assistants, and another 40% are open to exploring them. Users expect agents to drive the biggest impact in the arenas of task and process automation. Process automation is a primary area where organizations have reported lesser-than-expected benefits from existing generative AI initiatives. Relatedly, organizations are pursuing a mix of techniques to improve quality, relevancy and security of model outputs, including monitoring live responses (55%), fine-tuning (48%) and response caching (38%), all of which take on increased importance in the intricate world of agents and agent management, as these entities are increasingly autonomous and capable of taking self-directed action.

More organizations are getting a handle on key generative AI challenges, but cost is an increasing area of focus. Organizations continue to identify major challenges around generative AI. The most commonly identified challenges relate to data privacy (38%), security risks (38%) and costs (37%). However, fewer organizations cite security risks as a challenge relative to a year ago — the figure shows a 7-point decrease from the prior survey — and organizations also appear to be more effective in identifying use cases and addressing content diversity. While some concerns such as training data availability and sustainability saw slight elevation, the overall pattern was positive, with many key challenges declining year-on-year. However, cost is becoming an increasing concern for many organizations. Not only has there been a slight year-on-year increase in how often cost is cited as a challenge to generative AI adoption, it has also emerged as the most commonly identified decision-making factor in AI project prioritization. Additionally, not only was cost the worst-performing project KPI, but assessments of performance appear to have worsened year-on-year.

Skills shortage remains a persistent challenge. In the year-ago survey, 10% of respondents indicated that skills shortages were the primary concern across their AI initiatives, making it the most poignant issue identified in the survey. This year, the percentage of organizations recognizing it as a concern remains consistent at 27%, meaning it is still a prevalent challenge. However, the proportion who consider it the top concern slightly decreased to 8%, as issues related to budget constraints, accuracy and privacy have gained attention. The impact of skills shortages varies across sectors; it is particularly pronounced in manufacturing, telecommunications, construction and real estate. These industries also report notably lower returns on investment from AI initiatives, suggesting that skills shortages are a significant obstacle to success. In response, organizations are diversifying their strategies to address this challenge. Approximately half of organizations facing skills shortages are focusing on reskilling or upskilling their current workforce (49%), while a similar proportion are turning to IT integrators and consultants (46%) to bridge the gaps.

Investment in MLOps continues to increase, with organizations enhancing measurement practices. Although AI project performance has not notably improved, the number of criteria used to measure AI performance has increased. Organizations on average report using 7.1 of the 20 AI measurement metrics considered in the survey, versus 6.3 in the prior-year survey. Despite performance improvements in areas such as inference time and frequency of usage, fewer criteria overall are deemed "very successful" compared to a year ago, with worsening scores related to risk and cost metrics. The increase in the number of measurement techniques may partly reflect incremental improvements in MLOps investment. The proportion of organizations that have invested in MLOps tools increased from 24% to 27% year-on-year, and a further 42% expect to start using an MLOps tool over the next 12 months. The main reasons why organizations have not invested in MLOps relate to limited existing AI usage and difficulty in proving the ROI of such investments

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Papa John's: Baking A Comeback
data.description:
I've had my eye on Papa John’s (PZZA) for seven months now, and this turnaround is finally starting to smell like it’s almost out of the oven.

Told you the shift to value would eventually pay off, didn’t I? Nothing hits better than a well-executed barbell strategy paired with streamlined first-party platforms—guest repeatability is back on the rise, and value perception is getting stronger by the quarter.

In my last write-up here I reiterated my 'Buy' rating, and we ended up with a total return of over 0.7%, underperforming the S&P which delivered just over 1.7% in the same period.

Of course, if we go back to my last 'Strong Buy', just before Irth Capital showed a possible interest in acquiring this pizza company, we had a total return of almost 3.5%, outperforming the S&P which delivered a return of almost -1.18%.

It's like I told you, before the $40s you had a nice bit of margin of safety to work with and an upside of up to 60% up to my price target of $64. Above that, close to $45, where we are now, the upside is just over 42%. Lower, but also quite attractive (even more so if you take into account the dividend yield of over 4%).

We can have some fun trying to guess the bottom — and hey, it’s part of the game. But with this kind of upside on the table, the exact timing doesn’t matter all that much in the end.

Remember, my call on Papa John's came at a time when we were hunting for great restaurant stocks that were either in their worst phases or just hurt by the macro environment of the 'Trump Tariffs'.

This was the case with some successful calls on Cracker Barrel (CBRL), MTY Food Group (MTY:CA), Restaurant Brands International (QSR), among other 'Ugly Ducklings of 2024'. All of these we have double-digit upsides in our pockets.

Other 'Ugly Ducklings', such as Dine Brands (DIN) and El Pollo Loco (LOCO) are in the red, but I am confident that we are about to find a bottom.

Do not confuse these 'Ugly Duckling' theses with other more opportunistic ones that we are making, such as those on Kura Sushi (KRUS) and Shake Shack (SHAK), in which we are not betting on a turnaround, but rather buying growth stories at the bottom.

That said, what’s Papa John’s been cooking up lately?

A Slice of Turnaround

You can probably guess that they crushed it in Q1. The stock had been bleeding in April due to macro headwinds, complete silence on the proposed acquisition by Irth (the reason for the rally in February) and, mainly, weak results from peers within the United States.

Domino's Pizza (DPZ) saw its same-store sales fall 0.5% year-over-year in Q1, well below the consensus that it would grow by 0.7% in the same period. In addition, the 'King of Pizza' stumbled on revenue, missing estimates by at least $10 million.

Pizza Hut, the pizza chain owned by Yum! Brands (YUM), had an even worse result. They extended their streak of negative same-store sales for the fifth consecutive quarter, sequentially accelerating the decline to 2% year-over-year in Q1. This weakness at Pizza Hut dragged down Yum!’s results, also missing revenue estimates for the quarter.

Even the frozen pizza game — which crushed it during the pandemic (thanks to those income transfer programs that made Papa Murphy’s pizzas boom) — is now taking hits on both sides of the border.

If you read my articles on chain restaurants, you’ll notice that this scenario is in stark contrast to what we’ve seen at full-service restaurants, especially casual dining.

Texas Roadhouse (TXRH), Darden Restaurants (DRI), Cracker Barrel, Chili’s (EAT), BJ’s Restaurants (BJRI) and many others performed very well in the first quarter—despite wildfires, blizzards and a sharp drop in consumer confidence.

This appears to be a continuation of the ‘Fast-Food Exodus’ phenomenon, with limited-service restaurants (mostly fast-food joints) failing to deliver the value-per-dollar that diners are looking for.

The food simply isn’t worth it.

Now, this logic applies to all diners — but it hits hardest for those on tighter budgets. If they’re going to splurge on eating out, they’re more likely to pick a full-service spot offering prix fixe deals. A few extra bucks for way better food and actual service? Kind of a no-brainer.

Want it in numbers? Okay, here we go. According to RMS, traffic to limited-service restaurants sank 0.7% year-over-year in Q1, a sequential decline from where traffic was growing by nearly 1% in the much-talked-about Q4.

You can clearly see that the guest has gone into defensive mode when you look at the very weak breakfast traffic. As I’ve said in previous analyses, this is the most discretionary time of day for the guest.

They can certainly prepare their meal at home or head to a grocery store or c-store.

To give you an idea, breakfast traffic sank 9.5% year over year in Q1. I warned you that daytime dining was definitely not my bet in Q1. Restaurants like Denny’s (DENN), First Watch (FWRG) and even Wendy’s (WEN) tanked.

It was in this peculiar scenario that Papa John's reported its Q1 FY 2025 results.

And as I said before, you can probably guess from the chart without even reading the earnings report that they reported better-than-expected results.

The stock was trading at $33.30 before the bell rang on May 8. Right after Papa John's double-beat, the stock soared more than 14% to over $38. In the days that followed — and even now — the stock just kept climbing.

It’s already up almost 30% from where it was before the earnings drop.

In Q1 FY 2025, Papa John’s reported systemwide sales of $1.22 billion, up just 1% year over year. But let’s not get ahead of ourselves — those are the combined sales from both company-owned and franchised locations. If we really want to understand what’s cooking under the hood, we’ve got to start with revenue.

Papa John’s reported revenue of $518.3 million in the quarter.

That 1% revenue bump year over year came from a mix of small but meaningful wins: two net new restaurant openings in North America, a 3% increase in international same-store sales, and higher marketing contributions and commissary sales.

That growth was partially offset by a drop in the number of international restaurants following the restructuring of operations in the UK and a drop in same-store sales of 2.7% year-over-year in the US.

But if same-store sales continue to decline, why does the smell of turnaround remain in the air?

  1. While other pizza chains saw a sequential decline in same-store sales, Papa John’s saw a significant 330 bp improvement. And the best part is that this is driven by traffic growth, not price increases that would create tailwinds to the average check. This is a result of a focused approach to increasing transactions on 1P platforms (by making the royalty structure of its Papa Dough loyalty program more accessible, favoring repeatability) and continually offering value platforms like Papa Pairings (now more than ever focused on its core pizza business);
  2. They're pulling off a bit of a marketing magic trick—boosting brand perception while quietly drifting away from their old 'Better Pizza, Better Ingredients' identity. Honestly, I thought this would be way messier after Shambura (their former CMO) left. Especially with competitors like Domino’s throwing out gimmicks like the 'Emergency Pizza,' even though, fun fact, Papa John’s still wins in blind taste tests;
  3. They continue to perform well on special occasions. Those who remember my early write-ups may recall that I said that Papa John's was performing very well on special occasions, with premium tickets coming in on dates like Valentine's Day, Mother's Day, etc. More recently, Halloween 2024 was the biggest sales day in Papa John's history, showing that they continued to be top of mind when you want pizza on a special day. This continued in Q1 with the Super Bowl and Valentine's Day;
  4. Cutting back on specific menu items like Papa Bowls and some desserts (like the Cinnapie) to focus on pizza is starting to pay off. The number of pizzas ordered increased 4% year-over-year, including a sequential improvement. Items like the New York-style pizza, Shaq-a-Roni and the new $13.99 Epic Stuffed Crust pizza are starting to gain more prominence again. It also frees up space for different innovations like the Jack-o'-lantern Pizza (a pumpkin-shaped pizza they launched for Halloween) and the Star Pizza, which has already launched in the UAE.

To top off the turnaround, Papa John’s gave us some good news for Q2 FY 2025. In the first five weeks of Q2 (April and early May), Papa John’s saw same-store sales decline by just 1% year-over-year.

That’s a sequential improvement of at least 170 bp!

And again, best of all, this was driven by traffic that grew 1% year-over-year in the same period.

However, it’s not all roses. Papa John’s had to temporarily compromise on one very important thing: margins. If you look at adjusted EBITDA in Q1 FY 2025, it was $50 million, which is down at least 18% year-over-year. The reason? Higher SG&A. But not just a little bit higher. It was $62.1 million in the quarter (about 11.98% of revenue and up almost 28.5% year-over-year).

This happened for two reasons:

  1. An incremental marketing investment of at least $7 million to reinforce the value proposition with the 'Meet The Makers' campaign and continue to run 'Better Pizza, Better Ingredients';
  2. Approximately $4 million spent on biannual franchisee conference.

This also caused EBIT to plummet from $43.3 million to $27 million in the quarter (a margin of approximately 5.2% versus 8.4% last year). But note, if SG&A had been in line with last year, EBIT would have been approximately $40.9 million (only 5% lower year-over-year).

Since these are non-recurring expenses, this is not the time to worry about them. The important thing is that the brand is seeing sequential growth in its value scores, and that is what matters in the long run.

Remember Cracker Barrel not charging extra for egg dishes and even doubling Cracker Barrel Rewards points? Profitability doesn’t always speak louder in the short term.

With lower interest on debt, Papa John's reported net income of $9.2 million, down about 37% year over year. Diluted EPS was $0.27.

Even with lower net income year over year, Papa John's reported OCF of $31.3 million (up 160% year over year, less affected by operating asset headwinds). With Capex of $12.2 million, FCF was $19.1 million. This was more than enough to pay for the $15.2 million in dividends and buybacks of just over $1.1 million ($16.3 million returned to shareholders).

Also in financing, they rolled over $200 million in debt stretching it out over two years. No net effect on debt.

At the end of the quarter, they held $741.9 million in long-term debt (net debt of $697.9 million) and a Debt/EBITDA ratio of 2.98x, which is nothing to worry about.

Speaking of restaurant development, Papa John’s ended Q1 with 6,019 restaurants globally. In North America, they opened 18 restaurants and closed 16 (net 2 new openings in the period), bringing their NA restaurant footprint to 3,516 (58% of the chain’s total restaurants). In the International segment, Papa John’s opened 29 restaurants and closed 42 restaurants (net closures of 13 restaurants), bringing their segment footprint to 2,503.

For the entire FY 2025, they expect to open 85 to 115 gross restaurants in North America. As management expects, they will have closures of 1.5% to 2% (52 to 70 restaurants).

In the International segment, they plan to open 180 to 200 gross new restaurants in our international markets. With closures expected to be 4% to 5% of the system for the year (100 to 125 restaurants).

Even with the hit to the bottom line, this is more of a win than a loss. Papa John’s managed to outperform the rest of the pizza pack this quarter—and after several rocky ones, that’s worth celebrating.

It really kicked off when they started nudging guests toward first-party channels and reworked the royalty structure. That might sound like inside baseball, but for the pizza industry, it's a big deal—and clearly, it’s starting to pay off.

Proof of this is that the guidance for FY 2025 that I showed you in my last article has been reiterated.

Some New Faces in Thesis

Now that we've had our fill of Papa Doughs, 1P channels, and value menus, let's turn the page and meet some fresh faces in the 'Papa Thesis' lineup.

First up: management made it pretty clear they're looking to refranchise some of the restaurants in their portfolio. Papa John's currently has 539 restaurants in North America (that's less than 15% of their national footprint, which makes Papa John's an asset-light restaurant chain).

It's all still very fresh, and if they pull this off, expect improved margins at the corporate level and lower revenue inflows.

Another interesting development was the partnership with Google Cloud (GOOG) (GOOGL) to leverage digital opportunities.

With many customers migrating to 1P platforms (about 70% of orders are now placed on these platforms, with Papa Rewards having over 37 million active members), Papa John's will be able to use data to its advantage.

I know that sometimes they talk a lot about AI (often inappropriately) and it ends up becoming a nothingburger.

Let me give you some examples of what they can do with this technology integrated into their own channels:

  • The classic AI-powered recommendations and promotions for 1P channel users based on previous orders. In the case of the partnership with Google Cloud, they can use Vertex AI to deploy machine learning models at scale. If I’m not mistaken, Dine Brands deployed something similar with IHOP a while back;
  • They can boost delivery efficiency by optimizing driver routes and providing real-time traffic updates through Google Maps APIs;
  • Use Google Cloud's Dialogflow to create a chatbot integrating support solutions;
  • Use Google Cloud’s business intelligence tools to track sales trends and customer preferences. This should help them streamline the menu.

I'll keep an eye on it. These are solutions that could continue the trend of increasing guest satisfaction, but for now there's not much more to say about it.

Still 'Buy' With +46% Upside

As you can see, for the first time in a while, Papa John's finally pulled ahead of its direct competitors. This smells to me like the beginning of a turnaround.

As I mentioned in my last write-up, the Irth acquisition — even at a hefty premium — would be a nice bonus here. But I’m not counting on it being the star of the show.

Business seems to be getting back on track, with international same-store sales growing in the low to mid-single digits year over year. Latin America, Spain, the Middle East, and finally the UK are all showing some momentum.

Same-store sales should also be flat in North America as traffic growth offsets headwinds to average checks caused by the change in Papa Rewards’ royalty structure. Add that to the marketing investments, and I’m expecting some good momentum through the end of the year.

Keep in mind, the price target’s sitting around $64, which means there’s about a 46% upside. Not too shabby, right? Plus, they’re still handing out a solid 4.22% dividend yield.

So if this turnaround plays out, and they catch up with the rest of the industry by year-end, we're looking at roughly a 50% total return. Pretty sweet deal.

I maintain my 'Buy' recommendation here.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Salesforce: Market's Obsession With Growth Presents An Opportunity
data.description:
After the initial positive reaction in the early post-market trading, the market reaction to Salesforce, Inc.'s (CRM) Q1 2026 results has suddenly changed. The stock ended the day in red as investors' sentiment towards the stock remains mixed.

The same story has happened over the past year or so as CRM was initially outperforming the Nasdaq Composite Index, just to come crashing down over the past 6-month period. On one hand, this performance is expected given CRM's 5-year beta of around 1.4, but on the other it comes as a result of the company's transition into a more mature business.

I have been increasingly optimistic about CRM's future given its adoption of the "Oracle Playbook" of heavier reliance on organic growth and focus on profitability. However, with the acquisition of Informatica and other smaller deals over the past year, CRM's management seems to be recognizing that the current ecosystem of services is not competitive enough. For that and some other reasons, I have been cautiously optimistic CRM even though I rated the stock as a Buy.

I remain cautiously optimistic about the stock's performance in 2025 as continued reliance on acquisitions deviates from the "Oracle Playbook" for profitable organic growth.

Source: Seeking Alpha

While this is disappointing, I would expect CRM to rely on smaller bolt-on acquisitions, instead of very large deals that rarely create value for shareholders, it should be mentioned that significant progress is being made in terms of GAAP profitability. At the same time, the stock is priced more attractively, which is a downside risk at a time when the equity market is near record-highs.

Progress On Profitability

On a Non-GAAP forward earnings basis, Salesforce is now priced just slightly above the sector median.

Moreover, within its expanded peer group, CRM has become one of the cheapest large cap cloud software stocks. While we should consider that there are non-GAAP figures, CRM also has the third-highest gross margin within the peer group below, after Adobe (ADBE) and Intuit (INTU) - two companies that operate in more specialized areas of the market.

The two major adjustments to CRM Non-GAAP earnings are the annual amortization of intangible assets and the stock-based compensation expense. The former is not as important given it is highly discretional on management assumptions, but at the end of the day, it is an indication of how aggressive the company is in the M&A space - something that I have been critical of.

The company's reliance on equity-based compensation packages has been a cause of concern given the large shareholder dilution within the process. In recent years, however, this expense has gone down on a relative basis and is now at record lows when compared to the company's annual sales.

More importantly, Salesforce's management has proven to investors that they could make meaningful progress on streamlining the business and taking advantage of economies of scale. Marketing and sales expenses are by far the largest cost item for the company and as we could see on the graph below this has fallen sharply as a share of revenue.

Lower Downside Risk

Thus, Salesforce GAAP operating margin is now at a record-high of nearly 20% - something that seemed impossible just a couple of years ago.

In the meantime, the recent drop of the share price has now resulted in a price/sales multiple of 6.6, which is the lowest it has been since 2017 if we exclude the bear market of calendar year 2022 (FY 2023).

The current guidance for FY 2026 assumes operating margin to continue improving and to reach almost 22% for the year.

Thus, from a historical point of view, CRM is attractively priced and is no-longer exposed to downside movements to the same extent it was a couple of years ago.

Investors, however, are punishing the stock as topline growth has slowed-down meaningfully from above 20% to approximately 8% based on the current guidance. Normally, this leads to momentum-seeking investors gradually reducing their exposure, but for long-term investors, this is good news as long as CRM remains on its current path.

The lower market exposure (beta) is yet another sign that CRM is not driven by momentum trade to the same extent that it used to be.

Investor Takeaway

Although investors were not impressed with Salesforce's Q1 2026 results, the company is making good progress on its profitability targets and this matters for long-term investors. With momentum traders reducing their exposure to CRM, the stock also offers lower downside risk at a time when equities are at high risk of correction.

data.title: Banco BBVA Argentina: Forced To Face The Real Economy In Milei's Era
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Banco BBVA Argentina (BBAR) has grabbed investors’ attention with a huge rally following the political and economic shakeup under President Javier Milei’s administration. Since Milei’s win in late 2023, the stock has soared over 277%, putting it right in the middle of the bigger conversation about where Argentine banks are headed.

In the current environment, the bank has navigated a transformational macro backdrop, driven primarily by renewed interest in Argentina's capital markets and evolving expectations around risk and return. Along with its domestic peers, BBVA Argentina has moved away from a state-dependent model fueled by high-yield public instruments toward a traditional credit-driven model. Although this transition is necessary for long-term health, it also introduces execution risk in an environment that remains economically and politically fragile.

As a result, from 2024 onwards, paradoxically, financial margins and ROE have plummeted while valuation multiples have expanded significantly. Even so, macro tailwinds could continue to support BBAR in the short term, especially as global investors rotate out of overvalued U.S. assets and look to emerging markets. Argentine equities—despite their risks—remain attractive momentum plays.

In this light, BBAR's near-term upside may be less about its own fundamentals and more about investor sentiment, capital flows, and FX dynamics favoring Argentina. However, as it is very difficult to argue against the fact that BBAR is currently overvalued based on its profitability, and with less promising guidance for H2, I am refraining from taking a more bullish stance at the moment, but neither am I bearish.

Argentine Banks Finally Face the Real Economy Thanks to Milei

Before delving deeper into BBVA Argentina's thesis, I believe it is important to outline the current situation of the country's banks.

Starting with a step back, under the previous interventionist administrations of Kirchner and Fernández, the Argentine economy experienced persistent fiscal deficits, forcing the government and its central bank [BCRA] to rely on private domestic banks for financing.

The banks' response was to shift away from private-sector lending and instead channel deposits into state-issue bonds and Central Bank Instruments, such as LELIQs (liquidity bills).

These instruments were high-yield, low-risk in nominal terms, creating a system where banks profited without taking on real credit risk, but at the cost of becoming passive players in the economy.

In short, what happened was an atrophy of the traditional credit intermediation role—lending to businesses and supporting investment.

And soon the consequences came.

To finance deficits, the Argentine government often monetized debt, feeding chronic inflation. The BCRA tried to mop up excess pesos with remunerated liabilities, and banks received attractive interest rates. The outcome was indeed a reduction in inflationary pressure in the short term. But this created a new problem: a perverse incentive for banks.

Why would banks take risks lending to companies when they could earn much more and with much less risk just by leaving the money in the BCRA or buying government bonds?

So, banks stopped lending to the private sector and started living off short-term public assets, and the real economy lost out with less productive credit.

Until Javier Milei came along to take over the presidency of Argentina, setting a new fiscal and monetary direction.

Since taking office in 2023, the Milei administration has sought to reduce the fiscal deficit, dismantle the BCRA's quasi-fiscal deficit, and cut interest rates to weaken the appeal of passive instruments like LELIQs.

With these measures, Argentine banks are now less incentivized to rely on returns from government instruments. Even though sovereign bonds rebounded strongly in 2024, promoting a sharp increase in earnings due to improved overall sentiment with Milei's election, the trend is that this will be an unsustainable tailwind going forward.

Thus, Argentina's banking scenario has become at least less risky. In fact, Fitch Ratings recently upgraded Argentina's sovereign credit rating to ‘CCC+’—which is still a very low rating, despite a less critical situation than before—and in parallel, raised the Operating Environment [OE] score for banks to the same level.

The basis for these upgrades is the progress of the economy in terms of improved GDP per capita, falling inflation, and, clearly, this reduced dependence on state funding.

That said, these improvements are still fragile, as Argentina is still far from being a low-risk country.

Although inflation in Argentina is still high by global standards, at 47.3% in April (albeit cooling significantly compared to 84.5% in January), there are signs of improvement in the banking sector.

For example, non-performing loans are below 2% within the banking system, liquidity remains solid, while banks have returned to private-sector lending.

Looking ahead, as easy government-driven profits disappear, Argentine banks must now act as commercial banks. They will need to develop credit assessment capabilities, rebuild risk processes, and above all, reconnect with corporate and small business clients, who have been neglected for many years.

The challenge is now to face the real economy, which involves a more risky and complex environment, adjust their models to potentially lower but more sustainable margins, learn to manage credit risk, and above all, regain the trust of borrowers.

BBVA Argentina’s Hot Streak Cools Off But Still Has Some Steam

After a fantastic year in 2024 for Argentine equities, in which BBVA Argentina saw a significant increase in value of 250% during the period, the bank's performance in the first five months of 2025 has been poor, with a total increase of only 4.8% to date.

The recent weaker performance has highlighted the overall challenge facing the Argentine banking sector in rebuilding its business model after potentially most of the gains from the post-Milei macroeconomic shift have already been accounted for.

The latest results reported in Q1, on May 22, show quarterly equity returns [ROE] are still low at 11. 4% (vs. past levels of 21.5% reached in Q3 2023, for example) for a bank trading at a price-to-book ratio of 1.7x or a forward P/E ratio of 11.6x.

As BBAR suffers significantly from falling interest rates, which reduce net interest margin, in 1Q225, the bank reported a 23.5% year-over-year decline and a sequential decline of 8.6%.

However, not everything has been bad.

In the annual comparison, the scenario is one of progress, at least for ROE and ROA.

Quarterly ROE jumped from 6.6% in 1Q24 to 11.4% in 1Q25, and quarterly ROA stood at 2%, a significant jump of 40bps versus 1Q24, especially in a sector with tight margins such as banking.

In the Argentine context, where the banking sector has experienced distortions and compressed margins, this is a positive sign of adaptation.

Moreover, for more significant ROA growth, the solution for BBVA Argentina would be to increase leverage through growth in peso deposits (because the margin in dollars is very low).

In the specific case of BBVA Argentina, deposits grew more than at other banks, but they are still starting from a very low base. In the year-on-year comparison, peso deposits rose 47% and leverage went from 3.9x Assets/Net Equity to 5.9x. Private loans grew 123% year-over-year in the same period.

The crux here is that this kind of strategy may be fragile and risky, especially in a macro environment that is still volatile.

Credit growth is positive, but if it is not accompanied by adequate risk analysis, it can compromise the bank's future profitability.

The difference was financed by ending repurchase agreements with the BCRA, which does not increase leverage or improve margins (it is merely the exchange of one asset for another), but caused the capital ratio to fall more than proportionally. Even so, BBVA Argentina still has a very low default rate, and there was an improvement in the efficiency ratio (operating cost divided by operating income) for the quarter and the year.

In short, there haven’t been meaningful structural improvements in BBAR's bullish investment thesis at this time. I believe that sustained gains close to triple digits year-on-year still reflect market optimism linked to capital flows into Argentine equities and the appreciation of the Argentine peso against the US dollar.

This is confirmed, judging by the 2025 guidance, which has been revised downward in terms of loan growth (to 45% from 50% for the year) and deposits by 25% in real terms. The ROE guidance for the year points to the mid-teens and is biased toward the low teens. In other words, we should not see any major improvements over what was seen in 1Q25.

The Risks Are Clear, But So Is the Macro Tailwind

Considering that BBVA Argentina trades at a price-to-book multiple of 1.7x and has an ROE of 11.4%, the implied return to shareholders based on the ROE/PB ratio would be only ~7%.

I emphasize “only” because, while this level of return may seem reasonable for developed markets, it is clearly below the cost of capital required for Argentine assets, which is very difficult to estimate given the volatility of inflation in the country and the real country risk rates (high and unstable), making assumptions fragile. However, I do not believe that this figure could be less than 18% today.

In my view, BBAR's ADR is currently overvalued based on its current profitability, unless the Argentine bank manages to rapidly expand its ROE or drastically reduce its country risk—both scenarios that I consider uncertain or very long-term.

Of course, this does not mean that this will lead to prolonged weak performance of the ADR in the second half of the year.

I believe that above any operational or profitability indicator within or close to guidance, BBAR should continue to trade upwards as Argentina's recovery makes progress. BBVA Argentina's expectation is that economic activity will expand by around 5.5% in 2025 after a decline of 1.7% in 2024. In addition, throughout this year, there has been a clear migration of investment flows out of the U.S. market and into other markets around the world, including emerging markets, as well as China and Europe.

The movement has been predominantly macroeconomic so far, as a short-term consequence of the rise of protectionism in the United States. It is worth remembering that U.S. stock markets had been trading at prices well above their historical average, which, in an uncertain environment with the possibility of lower expected growth due to the impact of tariffs on the US economy, facilitates the migration of flows to other risky assets around the world.

In this context, although Argentina's equities have already capitalized on a strong appreciation in 2024 and are not necessarily discounted from a technical point of view, the trend is that they may benefit from this flow in some way.

Final Remarks

BBVA Argentina reported a better 1Q25 than previous quarters, despite still showing some structural weaknesses, such as a forced transition to private credit without a culture or systems in place, reflecting low-volume operations in relation to equity, which reduces the multiplier effect of profits—unless there is more leverage, which would be very risky in a still fragile economic environment.

Given this scenario, it has been challenging for BBVA Argentina, as well as other domestic banks, to grow volumes and maintain margins close to previous years.

Thus, after massive appreciation since Milei's election, current profitability would not justify the current price of BBAR's ADR. However, I do not rule out a still bullish performance in H2, much more anchored by a greater flow of capital leaning toward emerging markets and better FX expectations, and not necessarily by an improvement in the Argentine bank's fundamentals.

data.title: FDL: A Deep Dive Into Yield, Safety, And Timing
data.description:
The First Trust Morningstar Dividend Leaders Index Fund ETF (FDL) tracks the Morningstar Dividend Leaders Index, which includes stocks with the highest dividend yields and a consistent history of sustained dividend payments over the past 5+ years.

FDL selects the top 100 US stocks based on indicated forward dividend yield. It includes companies that have paid consistent dividends for at least five years. Filters are applied to check for dividend sustainability such as unsound payout ratios or weak financial health, avoiding dividend traps. Stocks must also meet minimum size and liquidity requirements. What makes FDL distinct is that it weights holdings by total dividends paid (in dollar terms), rather than market cap or yield, giving greater emphasis to large, consistent dividend payers. This results in a high yield, value tilted ETF that favors defensive sectors like consumer staples, energy, and financials. The focus is clearly on income payout that aggressive growth.

Holdings

The sectoral split is refreshing. A rule-based allocation that ends up in such defensive positioning is theoretically what income ETFs should look for. A dividend focused ETF scores over other kinds of income ETFs in theoretically being market-agnostic and preserving capital growth potential in the longer haul. Option writing ETFs are either too aggressive, writing options on volatile underlying and aiming for aggressive payouts or simply tracking a larger index with tech emphasis. For FDL, the value and defensive tilt is evident in the top ten holdings as well.

Yield and Total Return

Yield numbers are not super aggressive, but significantly high and steady for a defensive income seeker. As value and defensive stocks have taken a back seat in the past few years - the years of bull run in big tech and high growth, dividend yields have grown for defensives (lower share prices inflating the dividend yield numbers). The yield will look even higher in periods of corrections as that seen in the pandemic era. Of course, yield jumps like these are not in dollar terms.

The better measure of performance is total returns. I have compared FDL against a higher beta ETF like SPY and another active dividend paying ETF (compared to FDL's passive methodology-based approach). I am expecting the dividend ETFs to perform better in crashes, but behaviors in 2020 looks different from the tariff sell-off recently. Also, I do not see any evident pattern between SCHD and FDL. We will need to zoom in to see if historical performance data indeed throws up what is expected theoretically.

For corrections, I will zoom into the COVID crash, the corrections in 2022 and the recent tariff sell-off. For bull rallies, I will zoom into post COVID crash to 2022 and 2024.

The below total returns for the 2020 crash behaves quite erratic. SCHD expectedly did better than a non dividend focused growth index like SPY, but only by a small margin. FDL surprisingly fell more than SPY.

In the 2022 corrections though, the dividend ETFs did far better and by a margin. FDL was almost unaffected.

Similar behavior was observed in the recent tariff sell-offs too. Probably, FDL (and dividend ETFs in general) suffer more when markets move from a consolidation phase (as was pre-2020) to corrections. A cooling-off correction after a bull rally is where the dividend ETFs perform, FDL more so.

Below are the two bull market scenarios. Growth approach-based SPY outperforming is a given. However, in the first leg of the rally post pandemic, SCHD did far better than FDL. In 2024, roles reversed.

Just going by empirical observations, FDL is best suited for cooling-off post rallies (i.e. pullbacks vs. crashes) and late-stage rallies. It does not do well in crashes post consolidating markets and rebounds from there.

Case for FDL Ahead

I started out liking the rules-based passive and transparent nature of FDL, compared to actively managed dividend ETFs. However, I was expecting better volatility management from FDL. Particularly, performance in the COVID crash in 2020 is an overhang. Underperformance there sets investors back by years. Since FDL's proposition is for defensive income seeking investors, I will look at volatility management more than anything else. Its performance in late stage bull rallies or in pullbacks gets overshadowed by the one-off but telling underperformance in a crash from consolidating markets.

Moving on, if investors are more active and want to bet on the basis of what lies ahead (should not be for a pure income plan which should be market agnostic), then prospects for FDL improve. Any correction from here will be a pullback from a bull rally, a zone where FDL is comfortable in. If the rally resumes from here too, FDL works. The only time we will need to pull out from FDL and move into another defensive like SCHD is when markets have consolidated for a long time, say years. FDL looks safe for another couple of years at least.

All in all, it is important to remind oneself, why a defensive dividend ETF. We all know that growth ETFs will outperform in the very long run. Investors who let go of total returns, should at least get better volatility management, which FDL is not providing as promised and consistently. I will end up still rating FDL as a buy on two counts. One, FDL is a valid use case for yield focused investors prioritizing income over capital appreciation and also for defensive stabilization of a growth portfolio. Although I will recommend a non dividend paying value ETF for the stabilizing use case. Two, its recent performance is promising, post the pandemic glitch. Also, the markets now are looking at more risks from macro, tariffs, geopolitical tensions, economic slowdown and rates staying higher than longer, than any time in the past few years. FDL works here!

data.title: Catalyst: Solid Business Model Propels Growth For This Buy And Build Pharma
data.description:
This is my 9th Catalyst (NASDAQ:CPRX) article following my most recent 01/2025 "Catalyst Pharmaceuticals: The Stars Align For Flagship FIRDAPSE". In Stars Align I rated Catalyst as a "Strong Buy"; in the intervening months it has advanced a comfortable ~12%.

In this article I will check back to report how it is doing as per its Q1, 2025 earnings reported on 05/08/2025. This article references its Q1, 2025 earnings conference Call, 10-Q and its 05/12/2025 Corporate Presentation.

Catalyst operates an effective buy-and-build business model with three FDA approved products.

General

Catalyst is another one of those pharmas that operates without clinical stage molecules in development. Its current website pipeline shown below consists of three approved products that it acquired:

It acquired US rights to FIRDAPSE (amifampridine phosphate) in 10/2012 from BioMarin (BMRN) after it received EU approval; at the time it was in phase 3 development in the US. It acquired US rights to FYCOMPA (perampanel) CIII in 01/2023 from Eisai Co., Ltd. (Eisai).

Exclusive U.S. rights to AGAMREE (vamorolone) oral suspension 40 mg/mL reached Catalyst from Santhera Pharmaceuticals in 07/2023. It was FDA approved for use in treating Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy [DMD] in patients aged two years and older in 10/2023. Catalyst launched it in 03/2024.

FIRDAPSE

Presentation slide 8 below provides an overview of the current FIRDAPSE market opportunity in its approved indication Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome [LEMS]:

As indicated by the slide cancer-associated LEMS, which is thought to make up ~50% of the 5400 LEMS cases in the US, is under recognized in small cell lung cancer. This often leads to lengthy diagnostic journeys. It also creates challenges for Catalyst. It explains why there are currently 500 identified patients in LEMS diagnostic journey not yet treated with FIRDAPSE.

During the Call Catalyst noted FIRDAPSE revenue growth of ~25.3% in Q1, 2025. It emphasized its expectation for ongoing FIRDAPSE revenue growth of ~15% to 20% annually. It attributed its Q1, 2025 growth in excess of this expected range to temporary disruptions in Q1 2024 FIRDAPSE revenues attributable to the Change Healthcare cybersecurity incident.

CEO Daly indicated that the market offers an ~$1.2 billion opportunity for FIRDAPSE revenues at today's prices. He stated that Catalyst has penetrated about 25% of the available market which includes both idiopathic and cancer-associated LEMS.

Presentation slide 10 below provides color around its FIRDAPSE market opportunity. During the Call Catalyst reaffirmed net 2025 expected product revenue range for FIRDAPSE of between $355 million and $360 million.

FYCOMPA

Of Catalyst's three approved products, FYCOMPA is the least promising. As noted in Presentation slide 15 below Catalyst anticipates generic competition to FYCOMPA in 05/2025:

According to an 05/07/2025 Drugs.com entry no such generic was available at that time. During the Call CFO Kalb clarified the situation. He advised of Catalyst's expectation that:

...product revenue net for FYCOMPA will likely decrease once the patent protection expires, which occurs on May 23, 2025 for the tablets and on December 15, 2025 for the oral suspension.

During the Call CEO Daly advised that about 85% of patients are using the tablets.

CCO Del Carmen pegged full year 2025 FYCOMPA net product revenue forecast at between $90 million and $95 million. When asked about its potential once generic competition has had a chance to take hold in 2026 he responded:

...there tends to be a stickiness for lack of better terms for anti seizure medications. So there is an opportunity there for patients to or to minimize brand erosion over time. We haven't given that guidance as you mentioned for next year. But yes, I mean there is an opportunity to minimize brand erosion continuing into next year.

I am somewhat skeptical on this point. It is certainly going to depend on pricing and payer reaction to generic competition. The GoodRx website lists 30 tablets of FYCOMPA 4 mg as available at Walgreens for $1,178. If insurers remove FYCOMPA from their formularies while adding one or more generics instead, generics may prove to become quite a hurdle.

I think it would be rash to expect FYCOMPA to survive significant generic competition intact. Catalyst could easily experience significant erosions in its FYCOMPA revenues in 2026. Investors should consider this as a known unknown.

AGAMREE

While FYCOMPA lacks IP protection AGAMREE has comparably robust IP protection as shown by Presentation slide 12 below:

It is however far from ironclad, particularly after 04/2031. The 10-Q describes it in greater detail as:

In the U.S., AGAMREE® has New Chemical Entity exclusivity that expires in October 2028. AGAMREE® also has Orphan Drug Exclusivity expiring in October 2030. AGAMREE® is further protected by seven Orange Book listed patents expiring as early as May 28, 2029 and as late as July 16, 2040. The Company has also requested Patent Term Extension and will update the relevant expiration date in the Orange Book upon a final determination by the USPTO. The earliest a generic manufacturer could submit an ANDA is October 26, 2027. If we were to pursue a patent infringement action if any such ANDA challenges any of AGAMREE®’s Orange Book patents, then the automatic statutory 30-month stay would prevent FDA approval of such ANDA until April 26, 2031. [italics added]

Presentation slide 13 below shows the AGAMREE market opportunity:

This slide is not clear as to what it means by total addressable market for DMD population of >$1 Billion as regards AGAMREE. The footnote clarifies that it is not its 2025 guidance. During the Call CEO Daly gave the following 2025 AGAMREE guidance:

... early commercial indicators position AGAMREE for broader market reach as foundational complementary corticosteroid therapy within the evolving DMD treatment landscape. This momentum continues to support our full year 2025 net revenue forecast for AGAMREE between $100 million and $110 million.

This is certainly a positive indication for a therapy's second full year on the market. AGAMREE totaled ~$47 million in net sales from its 03/2024 launch through 12/31/2024. If you add its $22 million in Q1, 2025 revenues to this it comes to $69 million for its first full year revenues.

If it achieves its guided midpoint of $105 million for 2025 it will represent AGAMREE growth ~$36 million far above the expected FIRDAPSE growth.

Combined performance

Catalyst achieved strong top and bottom line beats for Q1, 2025. Its overall revenue of $141 million bettered analysts' expectations by $9.87 million. Its bottom line non-GAAP EPS of $0.68 beat expectations by $0.15.

Slide 23 below shows the relative growth dynamic among Catalyst's three approved products. FYCOMPA is expected to fall back after being leapfrogged by AGAMREE thanks to AGAMREE's superior IP protections:

Catalyst's superior financial profile earns it wide analyst support.

Presentation slide 20 below provides a concise overview of Catalyst's enviable financial profile:

Its year over year Q1 growth comparisons are skewed by the fact that its highest growing product AGAMREE was not fully launched until Q2, 2024. The slide is helpful in its report of $580.7 million cash and cash equivalents with no debt as of 03/31/2025.It is also significant in that it has positive Earnings Per Share Diluted (GAAP) of $0.19 for Q1, 2024 and $0.45 for Q1, 2025.

Its positive financial profile earns it a rare, for small pharmas, "Buy" recommendation from Seeking Alpha's metrics based quant system. Indeed the quant system's Ratings Summary PANEL is green across the board:

Wall Street analysts are particularly enthused by Catalyst's prospects. Seven of the eight Wall Street analysts covering the stock rate it as a "Strong Buy". The average price target for the eight analysts is $34 for an ~38% upside.

Summary

I rate Catalyst as a "Strong Buy". Its buy and build business model is proving to be highly effective. Its steady FIRDAPSE growth coupled with its more aggressive AGAMREE growth make for an attractive counter to its flagging FYCOMPA revenues.

Risks to this "Strong Buy" thesis are not hard to find. Most serious are those founded on IP issues. I have already discussed FYCOMPA's unfortunate IP vulnerability.

Its lead product FIRDAPSE has long been beset by competitive IP challenges. Catalyst has been successful in knocking these aside so far, however as reported in the 10-Q additional FIRDAPSE IP challenges keep popping up with several IP lawsuits pending.

AGAMREE is not immune from IP concerns despite its noted protection. Catalyst's latest 10-K (p. 18) indicates that patients transitioning to Sarepta’s (SRPT) Elevidys recently approved DMD gene therapy are likely to interrupt or postpone AGAMREE treatment. I do not expect this to be a scalable concern however it does illustrate the potential for unexpected IP hurdles.

data.title: Melexis NV: Speculative Buy On EV And Emerging Industry Trends
data.description:
Melexis NV (OTCPK:MLXSF) is a semiconductor company that develops mixed-signal ICs and sensors. It has a global footprint with 20 sites across three continents, with a portfolio that includes two segments. The first segment develops automotive products, such as ICs and ASICs for EV powertrains, thermal management, battery systems, e-braking/e-steering and advanced lighting. The second segment leverages its MEMS and silicon platforms in areas like micromobility, smart energy, robotics, and digital health. And it’s precisely this growth potential that makes MLXSF appear slightly undervalued at these levels. Thus, I rate the stock a compelling speculative “Buy.”

Semiconductor Technology Across Industries

Melexis NV is a technology company that develops mixed-signal integrated circuits [ICs] and sensors for automotive and industrial applications. They were founded back in 1988 and are currently headquartered in leper/Ypres, Belgium. Its operations span three continents and twenty strategic sites. You see, they have regional sales and application offices in Europe, Asia, and North America, as well as manufacturing installations in Belgium, Bulgaria, France, Germany, and Malaysia. As for its R&D centers, these are placed across Europe, Asia, and the Americas.

Broadly speaking, MLXSF has two segments, which are 1) automotive micro-electronics and 2) products for new markets outside traditional car electronics. The automotive segment includes several devices and application-specific integrated circuits [ASIC], such as EV powertrain, EV thermal, EV battery, E-braking, E-steering, and Lighting sensors. For example, EV powertrains include current amplifiers, voltage monitors, and gate driver ICs. These types of EV powertrains form the sensory and driving components that ensure performance, efficacy, and safety in an electric vehicle system.

Similarly, things like EV thermal management sensors and switches are necessary to maintain a safe operation of the battery pack and the automotive power electronics. These products include two main chip families: temperature sensors and control ICs to interpret sensor data and manage temperature-related actuators like cooling fans, heaters, among others. On the other hand, EV batteries are cell-monitoring ASICs to measure the voltage of each battery cell and protect ICs from dangerous conditions. Together, these two families of ASICs form an EV’s battery management system [BMS].

It’s also worth mentioning that MLXSF’s E-braking and E-steering actuator drivers and position sensors provide the power and control signals to the electric braking and steering motors. In other words, these types of systems replace hydraulic or mechanical systems. They eliminate bulky hydraulic lines and pumps, reducing weight and space in the vehicle. These devices also have position sensors to measure the exact position of a braking caliper or a steering rack. Alternatively, they can detect a column and ensure that the system always knows its position.

Lastly, the company also develops advanced lighting products like LED drivers to regulate current and voltage. These types of LEDs power vehicle lights, ambient-light sensing to measure light levels in the cabin for driver comfort, and energy saving. Alternative, advanced LEDs like these can interpret gesture control sensors like hand movements to activate infotainment, climate, and lighting adjustments.

And more recently, MLXSF is working on products for new markets beyond automotive. These are mostly adaptations of its micro-electro-mechanical systems [MEMS] and ASIC technologies to other domains. So, overall, MLXSF can provide broad applications for alternative mobility, such as E-bike drive electronics. Also, MLXSF produces ICs for a sustainable world, like sensing and power monitoring chips, for energy, environment, and smart home use cases.

But interestingly, this means MLXSF can also leverage its platforms to develop devices for robotics that are used to drive and sense motion in factory and service robots. Its technology can even be used to produce ICs that support wearable devices with low-power, high-precision sensors and in vitro diagnostics for blood-chemistry analyzers and portable lab-on-a-chip platforms. So, taking a step back, their products and technologies have ample use cases across several industries.

Tapping Mega-Trends for Growth

Having said that, MLXSF’s long-term growth strategy now hinges on interesting potential new applications within the automotive sector. Management noted they’ve identified three megatrends that it could tap into with its products, as they seem perfectly aligned. For example, in electrification, there is a shift from internal combustion engine [ICE] to battery electric vehicles and plug-in hybrid electric vehicle [BEV/PHEV] platforms. This shift demands more electronics, and MLXSF’s offerings could fit in nicely here.

The second trend they’re targeting is premiumization, where they could tap into by adding more comfort and convenience to the EVs and non-EV models. In this trend, they could basically just supply their mixed-signal ICs to help create those types of premium features. Lastly, the third trend is advanced driver assistance systems [ADAS]. ADAS in general try to make cars smarter and safer, so MLXSF’s sensing and driver ICs can facilitate its implementation.

By locking into these upcoming trends, MLXSF believes it can grow its automotive sales at a 10% CAGR (from 2024 to 2030). Note that this projection assumes zero growth in unit vehicle production globally, so the company outpaces the market by winning more content per car with some of its devices. In fact, it also means MLXSF’s market opportunity here is not dependent on substantial secular tailwinds.

Likewise, their newer products segment, beyond automotive sales, could grow at a 15% CAGR during the same period. This is also reasonable after considering that newer applications typically grow faster than more mature sectors. Also, the demand for applied ASIC platforms seems to be rapidly increasing as well in electrified micromobility, factory and service robots, smart energy, and health devices. So, from a bird's-eye perspective, they do seem to be well-positioned to tap into several growth trends.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, MLXSF trades at a $2.7 billion market cap, so it’s already a mid-sized operation in its sector. Its latest balance sheet from Q1 2025 shows they hold $37.7 million in cash and equivalents against $235.5 million in debt (aside from other regular operating liabilities). And their book value reached $623.4 million last quarter, which does imply a moderately high P/B of 4.3. For comparison, their sector’s median P/B is a bit lower at just 3.3. But, in all fairness, I think MLXSF’s growth potential across the sectors I previously outlined does warrant some premium relative to its industry.

For example, according to Seeking Alpha’s dashboard on MLXSF, they’re projected to generate $1.1 billion in revenues by 2026. That would price MLXSF at a forward P/S of 2.5, which is actually cheaper than its peers’ forward P/S of 2.8. So, I think that overall, their valuation is reasonable, if not a bit undervalued at these levels. However, I do estimate their Q1 TTM cash flow reached $137.2 million. Note that I got this figure by simply adding their cash flows from operations and Net CAPEX.

And while this shows they’re a self-sustainable operation that should be able to compound shareholder value, it does raise a yellow flag. After all, their Q1 TTM dividends paid were $161.7 million, which is slightly lower than my $137.2 million cash flow estimate over the same period. And in my view, it could be tricky to sustain such dividends while also trying to capitalize on growth trends, and having just $37.7 million in cash in their balance sheet. This could be a headline risk waiting to happen if they decide to cut their dividend going forward, or reduce it to make their growth efforts more sustainable.

But on the flip side, their TTM dividend yield is roughly around 6.5%, which is, by itself, very compelling for income investors. And if you add to it the company’s growth potential if their strategic priorities pay off, then I think MLXSF might be worth adding to your portfolio. This is why I believe that, on balance, the stock is worth a “Buy” for long-term investors.

Conclusion: Speculative “Buy”

Overall, MLXSF is a very promising play on the upcoming trends with EVs, as its technology seems to be a good fit for the applications needed for EVs in general. However, I have some concerns with its relatively low cash reserves and dividend policy, which might be challenging to sustain unless they deliver on their lofty growth ambitions. But, on the other hand, I think they’ve got a long history of success and have shown they can adapt to new technologies since 1988. Plus, the stock’s price tag appears a bit undervalued if its growth materializes, which is why I ultimately think it’s fair to rate it a speculative “Buy” for long-term investors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Private Equity Exits In Fossil Fuels Poised To Exceed 2024 Levels
data.description:
Global private equity exits in fossil fuels are on the rise and are about to surpass the transaction value reached in 2024.

Private equity exits in the oil and gas industry amounted to $18.54 billion across 17 deals between Jan. 1 and May 21, less than $1 billion away from the $19.41 billion amassed in full year 2024, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

Global PE/VC-backed exits in oil and gas

Yearly

Quarterly

Data compiled May 22, 2025.

PE/VC = private equity or venture capital.

* Year to date through May 21, 2025.

^ Quarter to date through May 21, 2025.

Analysis includes global whole-company acquisitions, minority stake acquisitions, and asset acquisitions, and rounds of funding announced between Jan. 1, 2021, and May 21, 2025, where the seller in the deal is or includes a private equity or venture capital firm, and where the target's primary industry is classified by S&P Global Market Intelligence as oil and gas.

Excludes terminated deals and add-on acquisitions.

Source: S&P Global Market Intelligence.

© 2025 S&P Global.

Private equity divestments in the sector followed the US administration's January executive order to prioritize the development of domestic energy resources, including oil and natural gas. The sector's exit activity bucks the trend in overall private equity exits, which fell to a two-year low in the first quarter.

Trade sales remained the most numerous type of oil and gas company exit in the first quarter, according to Preqin Pro data. IPOs were the second-most numerous private equity exit route.

North America and Europe accounted for virtually all private equity-backed exits in the oil and gas sector so far in 2025. The US and Canada had 13 exits totaling $15.93 billion, while Europe, including the UK, had three exits totaling $2.61 billion. Asia-Pacific had one private equity-backed exit in oil and gas, according to Market Intelligence data.

Largest private equity exits

The largest divestment in 2025 through May 21 was a $9 billion transaction in which KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR) and Caisse de dépôt et placement du Québec is selling their stakes in US refined oil pipeline operator Colonial Enterprises Inc. to Brookfield Infrastructure Partners LP.

The second-largest was Blackstone Inc.'s $1.8 billion sale of its 45% stake in Houston-based oil and gas gathering system operator Targa Badlands LLC to Targa Resources Corp. (TRGP).

Blackstone's (BX) pending $1.74 billion sale of certain upstream and midstream assets to US natural gas producer EQT Corp. (EQT) ranked third.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Fed Drops QT To $5B A Month - Ensuring The Balance Sheet Will Stay Bloated
data.description:
The following analysis breaks down the Fed balance sheet in detail. It shows different parts of the balance sheet and how those amounts have changed. It also shows historical interest rate trends.

Breaking Down the Balance Sheet

The Fed recently announced a further reduction in the balance sheet runoff from $25B a month to $5B a month. The data shows that they have been reducing the pace of runoff, but have not yet gotten down to the target $5B a month.

Figure: 1 Monthly Change by Instrument

The table below provides more detail on the Fed’s QT efforts. As shown, the current size of the balance sheet is $6.6T, which means it would take over 100 years to fully wind down their balance sheet at the new $5B pace.

Figure: 2 Balance Sheet Breakdown

The weekly activity can be seen below. As shown, it’s been mostly consistent with big chunks along the way. It is also allowing a range of maturities and products to roll off.

Figure: 3 Fed Balance Sheet Weekly Changes

The chart below shows the balance on detailed items in Loans and also Repos. These were the programs set up in the wake of the SVB collapse early last year. All of the programs have dropped down to zero at this point, showing that the emergency measures have come to a close.

Figure: 4 Loan Details

Yields

Yields have been fluctuating within a band since Sept 2022, ranging mostly between 3.25% and 4.75%. The 30-year has recently broken out of its range to the upside, signaling a lack of interest in US Government long-term debt.

Figure: 5 Interest Rates Across Maturities

The spread between the 2-year and 10-year has finally turned positive. When the inverted yield curve predicts recession, the recession does not usually occur until sometime after the spread turns positive. This ranges between 6 and 18 months. Considering we are hitting the 8-month point now, this could be a bad sign for the months ahead.

Figure: 6 Tracking Yield Curve Inversion

The chart below shows the current yield curve, the yield curve one month ago, and one year ago. It is clear to see the short-end coming down, while the long-end has remained mostly flat.

Figure: 7 Tracking Yield Curve Inversion

The Fed Takes Losses

When the Fed makes money, it sends it back to the Treasury. This has netted the Treasury close to $100B a year. This can be seen below.

Figure: 8 Fed Payments to Treasury

You may notice in the chart above that 2023 and 2024 are showing $0. That’s because the Fed has been losing money. According to the Fed: The Federal Reserve Banks remit residual net earnings to the U.S. Treasury after providing for the costs of operations… Positive amounts represent the estimated weekly remittances due to U.S. Treasury. Negative amounts represent the cumulative deferred asset position … deferred asset is the amount of net earnings that the Federal Reserve Banks need to realize before remittances to the U.S. Treasury resume.

Basically, when the Fed makes money, it gives it to the Treasury. When it loses money, it keeps a negative balance by printing the difference. That negative balance has just exceeded $229B!

Figure: 9 Remittances or Negative Balance

Who Will Fill the Gap?

The Fed has not been buying in the Treasury market for over a year (they have been selling); however, the Treasury is still issuing tons of new debt. Who has been picking up the slack since the Fed stepped away?

International holdings have increased a decent amount over the last year by over $750B. Unfortunately, this pales in comparison to the amount of debt issued by the Treasury overall, which is closer to $2T.

The recent increase in holdings is not shown in the detailed treasury report, classifying most of the increase as “All Other”. The countries that can be seen increasing holdings are Norway, Cayman Islands, UK, Switzerland, and Canada. On the other side, the biggest countries reducing debt are China, UAE, Hong Kong, and Ireland.

Note: data is updated on a lag. The latest data is as of March

Figure: 10 International Holders

It should be noted that China continues to reduce holdings of US Treasuries. They have dropped $500B over the last decade, while Japan has been mostly flat over the same time. The slack has been picked up by other countries.

Figure: 11 Average Weekly Change in the Balance Sheet

Historical Perspective

The final plot below takes a larger view of the balance sheet. It is clear to see how the usage of the balance sheet has changed since the Global Financial Crisis.

Figure: 12 Historical Fed Balance Sheet

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
